title: Summer heat protection for meat rabbits
subtitle: Rabbits suffer in heat far more than in cold; the kit that keeps a colony cool.
excerpt: Rabbits cope with British winter without complaint and collapse in a July heatwave. A rabbit's heat-tolerance ceiling is well below human comfort, and a hot hutch can lose stock inside an afternoon. This covers the cooling kit that matters: shade, airflow, frozen bottles, and the early signs of heat distress.
sourceNotes: Cornell Small Farms rabbit-keeping notes. RWAF welfare guidance on summer rabbit care. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A core body temperature above the safe range. In rabbits this means anything above forty-one degrees; rabbits cope poorly with even brief over-heating and can collapse fast.

content.text: A rabbit at twenty-eight degrees ambient is already in trouble. The thick fur coat that keeps her warm through a frost works against her in summer, and rabbits dump heat poorly: a few hot panting breaths, ear vessels dilated, and not much else in the way of cooling. A south-facing hutch in full afternoon sun in July is a heat trap that will kill a rabbit inside two hours. The kit that prevents that is simple and worth setting up before May.
Why rabbits suffer in heat
A rabbit's thermal comfort sits between ten and twenty degrees. Above twenty-five she begins to struggle; above thirty she is at risk of
hyperthermia

. Sweating is not an option: rabbits sweat only from the lips and the inside of the ear tips. The main heat-loss surface is the ears, with their dense network of blood vessels. A rabbit cooling herself stretches out flat, ears spread, on the coolest patch of floor she can find.
Siting hutches for summer

The single biggest factor is shade. A hutch under a tree, or shaded by the side of the shed for the afternoon hours, runs five to eight degrees cooler than the same hutch in the open. Move hutches into the shade in May; a portable run on a stand can be relocated in fifteen minutes. If the hutch is too heavy to move, hang a tarp or a hessian sheet over the south-facing side from May through September and lift it off at the autumn equinox.

Airflow matters almost as much. A hutch with mesh on three sides has cross-ventilation and stays markedly cooler than a closed-wall hutch with one wire front. The mesh need not be open all year; storm shutters can drop down in winter and lift in summer. A shed or barn housing the rabbits stays cool if the door and a window can both be opened to draught the room.
Frozen bottles

A two-litre plastic bottle filled with water and frozen overnight is the simplest cooling tool. Lay one on the floor of each hutch in the early morning of a hot day; the rabbit will lie against it and dump heat through her belly fur. Bring the bottles in at evening to refreeze for the next day. A working summer setup runs three bottles per hutch on a rotation: one in the freezer, one in the hutch, one being washed.

Ceramic floor tiles work the same way without the daily refreeze. A large unglazed tile placed on the hutch floor stays cool to the touch even in heat; the rabbit lies on it through the worst of the day. Run the tile through the dishwasher once a week and keep it dry.
Water

Water intake doubles in heat. A bottle-fed rabbit can drain a 500 ml drinker by lunchtime when she would normally take three days to empty it. Switch a bottle-fed colony to a heavier bowl in summer, or simply add a second drinker per cage. Check the bottle nozzles work freely; a sticky valve denies the rabbit water at the worst moment. A bowl in shade stays cool; a bowl in sun heats to a temperature the rabbit refuses.
Spotting heat stress

A rabbit too hot for comfort breathes fast through an open mouth; the nostrils flare and the chest works hard. Ears are held flat and the vessels are visibly dilated. She lies stretched out and reluctant to move. Wet around the mouth and nose is the next stage. A rabbit collapsed on her side, unresponsive, and twitching is at the end stage and will die without immediate cooling.

Cool an overheated rabbit by wetting her ears with cool (not cold) water from a sponge. Move her into a cool indoor room. Offer water. Do not plunge a hot rabbit into cold water; the shock can stop her heart. A rabbit that recovers within thirty minutes is probably safe; one that remains lethargic for more than a few hours after cooling needs a vet visit because internal damage may have happened.
Flystrike risk

Hot weather is also flystrike season. A rabbit with soiled fur around the back end attracts blowflies, which lay eggs that hatch into maggots within twenty-four hours. Check the back end of every rabbit twice a day from May through September; clean any soiled fur with warm water and dry the rabbit thoroughly. A rabbit with maggots visible is a vet emergency, not a wait-and-see.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Summer rabbit troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Most summer problems are caught at the morning round.

items.symptom: Rabbit lying flat, mouth open, breathing fast.
items.fix: Move the hutch into deeper shade now. Add a frozen bottle to the cage. Wet the ears with a sponge of cool water. Most rabbits recover within thirty minutes once the cooling kit is in place.

items.symptom: Drinker emptied by mid-morning every day in a heatwave.
items.cause: Normal hot-weather intake plus a possible leaking bottle.
items.fix: Add a second bottle or bowl. Check the bottle for a slow drip onto the bedding; a leaking valve loses water and dampens the cage.

items.symptom: Soiled fur around the back end after a wet weekend.
items.cause: Gut upset or overweight rabbit unable to clean herself.
items.fix: Wash the back end in warm water and dry thoroughly. Cut concentrate feed for two days. Add more hay to the diet. Check the back end again that evening and the next morning; soiled fur for forty-eight hours risks flystrike.

items.symptom: Rabbit unresponsive on her side, twitching.
items.cause: Severe hyperthermia; this is an emergency.
items.fix: Sponge the ears with cool (not cold) water. Move her to the coolest indoor room. Call the vet now. Most rabbits at this stage do not recover; the few that do owe it to immediate cooling.

content.text: Summer welfare for rabbits is an inversion of the usual instinct. The cold-weather kit (a deeply bedded hutch, an enclosed sleeping area, sheltered from draughts) is what kills in heat. Move the hutches in May, set up frozen bottles, double the water, and check the back end twice a day. The colony is then quiet through August and ready for breeding into the cooler weeks of September.
